function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Common-control recast accounting presents historical periods as if entities had always been combined. What investor-interpretability or transparency concerns arise when historical standalone performance becomes less observable?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the investor-interpretability and transparency concerns that arise when common-control recast accounting makes historical standalone performance less observable. This is a conceptual/analytical question about accounting treatment in the context of the SpaceX S-1 filing.

Let me first look at the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand how they've handled the common-control recast accounting, what disclosures they've made about it, and what specific concerns might be evident from the filing itself.

Let me parse the filing and then retrieve relevant sections about the common-control accounting treatment.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve information about the common-control recast accounting from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What does the SpaceX S-1 filing say about common-control recast accounting, basis of presentation, and how historical periods have been recast to include xAI and X Holdings? {{spacex_s1}}

What risk factors or disclosures does the SpaceX S-1 mention about the difficulty of comparing historical financial results, the impact of the xAI merger, or the recast accounting on investor understanding? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here is a detailed summary of how the company handles common-control recast accounting, its basis of presentation, and the retrospective recasting of historical periods to include xAI and X Holdings.

### 1. Basis of Presentation & Cautionary Preamble
*   **U.S. GAAP Compliance:** The consolidated financial statements are prepared in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), which requires management to make estimates and assumptions (e.g., revenue recognition, tax valuation allowances, inventory reserves, and fair value of intangibles).
*   **Cautionary Warning:** The preamble explicitly cautions that the summary historical consolidated financial and operating data **is not indicative of expected future results**, nor are interim results necessarily indicative of full fiscal year outcomes. 
*   **Directives to Readers:** The summary data is not intended to replace the full financial statements. Readers are directed to review this data in conjunction with the **"Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations" (MD&A)** and the audited consolidated financial statements and related notes included elsewhere in the prospectus.

### 2. Common-Control Recast Accounting
The filing details a series of mergers: xAI's acquisition of X Holdings and X.AI Corp. (effective March 28, 2025), followed by SpaceX's acquisition of xAI (effective February 2, 2026). 
*   **Common Control Rationale:** These transactions are accounted for as reorganizations of entities under **common control** because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest and majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented.
*   **Accounting Mechanics:**
    *   **Historical Carrying Amounts:** The net assets of the entities are combined retrospectively at their historical carrying amounts.
    *   **No New Goodwill:** No new goodwill or other intangible assets are recorded as a result of these mergers.
    *   **Eliminations:** All historical related-party and intercompany transactions between the entities are eliminated in consolidation.
    *   **Change in Reporting Entity:** This retrospective combination constitutes a formal "change in reporting entity."
    *   **No Separate Financials:** Because the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization for all periods presented, separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided.

### 3. Recasting Historical Periods for xAI and X Holdings
To present a unified financial picture, historical periods have been heavily recast to reflect the mergers and subsequent corporate actions:

*   **Retroactive Share Conversions:** Historical common shares of X, X.AI Corp., and xAI are presented in the financial statements **as if they had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock** at the applicable exchange rates for all periods presented. 
    *   *X Merger Ratios:* X Holdings common stock converted to xAI common stock at a 2.776 ratio.
    *   *xAI Merger Ratios:* xAI common stock converted to SpaceX common stock at a 0.1433 ratio (on a pre-split basis).
*   **Shareholders' Equity Continuation:** The historical capital stock and shareholders’ equity reflect a continuation of SpaceX’s historical equity, combined with the historical equity of X and xAI, adjusted by the respective merger exchange ratios.
*   **Exception for xAI Preferred Stock:** Unlike common stock, xAI’s historical Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is **not retrospectively recast** on an as-converted basis. Because it was a legally distinct equity class prior to the merger, historical balance sheets continue to reflect it as outstanding preferred stock. Its conversion into SpaceX Common Stock is only recognized prospectively in the period the merger actually closed (Q1 2026).
*   **2026 Stock Split Adjustment:** On May 4, 2026, SpaceX executed a 5-for-1 forward stock split for its Class A, B, and C Common Stock. All historical share and per-share information, as well as the conversion rates of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock, have been **retroactively adjusted** to reflect this split across all periods presented in the prospectus.

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1, the prospectus addresses the themes of historical comparability, the xAI merger, and recast accounting primarily through **accounting disclosures and operational risk factors**, rather than framing "investor confusion" or "comparability" as a standalone business risk factor. 

Here is how the S-1 handles these specific areas:

### 1. Disclosures on Recast Accounting & Historical Comparability
While the text does not explicitly warn that investors will have "difficulty understanding" the financials, it includes several strict accounting caveats and structural disclosures that inherently impact how historical results are compared and interpreted:
* **Historical Data is Not Indicative of the Future:** The S-1 explicitly cautions that the "summary historical consolidated financial and operating data presented below is not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period," and that interim results do not necessarily indicate full-year expectations.
* **Change in Reporting Entity:** Because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X, the mergers are accounted for as reorganizations of entities under **common control**. The financials are retrospectively recast to combine the net assets at their historical carrying amounts, eliminating related-party transactions and recording no new goodwill. The S-1 explicitly notes that "this presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."
* **Bifurcated Equity Presentation (Retroactive vs. Prospective):** The recast accounting creates a mixed historical presentation that investors must navigate:
  * *Common Stock:* Historical share counts and per-share data for X, xAI, and SpaceX are **retroactively adjusted** to reflect the merger exchange ratios and a subsequent 5-for-1 stock split (effective May 2026).
  * *Preferred Stock:* Conversely, xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is **not retrospectively recast** on an as-converted basis. Because it was a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, it is presented in its historical legal form and its conversion to SpaceX Common Stock is only recognized prospectively in Q1 2026. 

### 2. Risk Factors Regarding the Impact of the xAI Merger
The S-1 outlines significant operational and execution risks tied directly to the acquisition of xAI and the formation of the company's new AI segment:
* **Early-Stage Maturity:** The S-1 notes that prior to the February 2026 acquisition, xAI was an "early-stage company." Consequently, the newly formed AI segment remains in a relatively early stage of organizational and operational maturity.
* **Integration and Execution Hazards:** The integration of xAI is described as "inherently complex, costly and time-consuming." The S-1 warns this could result in operational inefficiencies, delays, disruptions, increased costs, loss of institutional knowledge, and the diversion of management's attention.
* **Infrastructure and Scaling Vulnerabilities:** The AI segment's success relies heavily on scaling terrestrial and orbital AI compute infrastructure. The S-1 flags risks regarding the availability of power, AI processors, and critical telecommunications components, noting that shortages could materially harm the business.
* **Rapidly Evolving Industry:** The AI segment operates in a fast-changing landscape, subjecting it to intense competitive, macroeconomic, and regulatory risks (including evolving U.S. and foreign laws regarding AI and the X platform).

### Summary
The S-1 manages the potential for investor misunderstanding regarding historical financials via **technical accounting disclaimers** (e.g., noting the "change in reporting entity," the non-indicative nature of past data, and the split retroactive/prospective treatment of equity classes). Meanwhile, the formal **Risk Factors** are reserved for the tangible business dangers of the xAI merger, specifically the high costs, execution hazards, and early-stage vulnerabilities of integrating a nascent AI company into SpaceX's broader multiplanetary and connectivity operations.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve more specific information about the segment reporting, the financial performance of the individual segments, and any disclosures about standalone performance or the challenges of evaluating the combined entity.

arguments.prompt: What segment information is disclosed in the SpaceX S-1? How are the segments defined (Starlink, Starshield, Launch, AI segment)? What are the revenue and operating results for each segment? {{spacex_s1}}
What does the SpaceX S-1 say about related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X that were eliminated in consolidation? What intercompany arrangements existed before the merger? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, the company does not report "Starlink," "Starshield," and "Launch" as standalone financial segments. Instead, it operates and reports under **three primary reportable segments: Space, Connectivity, and AI**. Starlink, Starshield, and Launch are core products and service lines housed within these broader segments. 

Here is the breakdown of how the segments are defined and their respective financial and operating results.

### 1. Segment Definitions
*   **Space Segment (Launch & Development):** Designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship) to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space. Revenue is derived from **Launch Services** (fixed-price contracts for satellite, cargo, and crew missions) and **Launch & Development** (development of spacecraft and mission services for government agency space programs). 
*   **Connectivity Segment (Starlink & Starshield):** Operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites. It serves consumer, enterprise, and government customers. Key offerings include **Starlink Consumer Broadband**, **Starlink Mobile** (satellite-to-mobile texting/voice), Enterprise Solutions, and **Starshield** (a secure satellite network designed specifically for government and national security applications).
*   **AI Segment (Grok, X, & Compute Infrastructure):** Operates a vertically integrated AI platform. This includes the frontier Large Language Model (LLM) **Grok**, AI solutions for consumers and enterprises, **X** (the real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform), and massive AI computational infrastructure (e.g., the COLOSSUS II facility).

---

### 2. Revenue and Operating Results by Segment

#### **Space Segment**
*   **Q1 2026 Results:** Generated **$4,086 million** in revenue, a loss from operations of **$(657) million**, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of **$653 million**.
*   **Revenue Mix:** Revenue is split between Launch Services (recognized at a point in time; ~53% to 65% of Space revenue) and Launch & Development (recognized over time; ~35% to 47% of Space revenue).
*   **R&D Expenses:** Funded $930 million in Q1 2026 and $3,004 million in FY 2025, primarily driven by accelerated investments in the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program.
*   *Note on Growth:* Space segment revenue growth is intentionally lower than total company growth because internal business (deploying Starlink and AI infrastructure) absorbs most of the launch capacity.

#### **Connectivity Segment (Starlink / Starshield)**
*   **Key Operating Metrics:**
    *   **Subscribers:** Grew to **10.3 million** (Q1 2026) from 2.3 million in 2023.
    *   **ARPU (Average Revenue Per User):** $66/month (Q1 2026), down from $99 in 2023 as the subscriber base scales globally.
    *   **Network:** ~9,600 broadband and mobile satellites in LEO; median latency of ~25ms and median download speeds of 225 Mbps.
*   **Q1 2026 Results:** Generated **$3,257 million** in revenue, income from operations of **$1,188 million**, and Adjusted EBITDA of **$2,087 million**.
*   **FY 2025 Results:** Generated **$11,387 million** in revenue, income from operations of **$4,423 million**, and Adjusted EBITDA of **$7,168 million** (representing YoY growth of 49.8%, 120.4%, and 86.2% respectively).
*   **Revenue Drivers:** Consumer subscribers represent over 60% of revenue, with enterprise, government (Starshield), and Starlink Mobile acting as significant growth contributors.

#### **AI Segment (X, Grok, Compute)**
*   **Key Operating Metrics:**
    *   **Nameplate Compute Draw:** Scaled rapidly to **1 Gigawatt (GW)** in Q1 2026 (up from 0 GW in 2023 and 0.8 GW for FY 2025).
*   **Q1 2026 Results:** Generated **$818 million** in revenue, a loss from operations of **$(2,469) million**, and Adjusted EBITDA of **$(609) million**.
*   **FY 2025 Results:** Generated **$3,201 million** in revenue, a loss from operations of **$(6,355) million**, and Adjusted EBITDA of **$(1,237) million**.
*   **Financial Context:** The segment is in an earlier stage of development and is prioritizing aggressive growth and investment. Massive losses are driven by the depreciation of GPU hardware, cloud computing costs, and data center infrastructure expansions (e.g., COLOSSUS II).

---

### 3. Inter-Segment Dynamics & Capital Expenditures
A critical theme in the S-1 is the **vertical integration and cross-segment subsidization** that powers SpaceX's "innovation engine":
*   **Inter-segment Launch Accounting:** When the Space segment launches Starlink satellites, it **does not recognize inter-segment revenue**. Instead, those launch costs are capitalized as Property, Plant, and Equipment within the Connectivity segment and depreciated over time. Therefore, Space segment revenue *only* reflects external customer launches, masking the foundational strategic value it provides to the Connectivity and AI segments.
*   **Capital Expenditures (Q1 2026):** 
    *   **AI Segment:** $7,723 million (Massive buildout of compute infrastructure)
    *   **Connectivity Segment:** $1,332 million (Satellite manufacturing and network expansion)
    *   **Space Segment:** $1,052 million (Launch pads, Starship facilities, and hardware)
*   **Capital Expenditures (FY 2025):** Space ($3,832M) and Connectivity ($4,178M).

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here is what the document states regarding the consolidation of SpaceX, xAI, and X, as well as the related-party transactions disclosed:

### 1. Elimination of Related-Party Transactions in Consolidation
The S-1 explains that the acquisitions of X by xAI (the "X Merger" on March 28, 2025) and xAI by SpaceX (the "xAI Merger" on February 2, 2026) were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. Because Elon Musk held a majority voting interest and controlling financial interest in all three entities during the periods presented, the mergers were not treated as standard market acquisitions. 

Consequently, the S-1 states:
* **Retrospective Combination:** The consolidated financial statements reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts for all periods presented.
* **No New Goodwill:** No new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded as a result of the mergers.
* **Elimination of Intercompany Transactions:** **"All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."** This means any internal financial exchanges, revenue recognition, or expense sharing that occurred strictly between SpaceX, xAI, and X prior to the mergers were wiped out to present the three companies as a single, unified economic entity from a historical accounting perspective.

### 2. Intercompany Arrangements Before the Merger
**The provided text does not detail the specific internal commercial arrangements or contracts that existed strictly *between* SpaceX, xAI, and X prior to the mergers** (e.g., internal agreements regarding Grok's access to X's data, or xAI's use of SpaceX's infrastructure). Because these entities were under common control and are now consolidated retrospectively, those specific internal intercompany agreements are buried in the eliminated consolidation entries rather than itemized as external related-party transactions.

### 3. Disclosed Related-Party Transactions (External Musk Affiliates & Board Members)
Instead of internal SpaceX/xAI/X arrangements, the "Related Party Transactions" section of the S-1 focuses heavily on the financial relationships between the consolidated company and **Elon Musk’s other external ventures**, as well as **board members and executives**. The disclosed arrangements include:

**Transactions with Tesla:**
* **Commercial, Licensing & Support:** SpaceX obtained goods/services from Tesla ($11M in 2023; $4M in 2024; $144M in 2025). xAI obtained goods/services from Tesla ($191M in 2024; $506M in 2025) and recognized minor revenue from Tesla.
* **Advertising:** Tesla purchased advertising on the X platform ($0.5M in 2024; $4M in 2025).
* **Aircraft Usage:** SpaceX invoiced Tesla for the use of SpaceX-owned aircraft by Musk and Tesla personnel for business travel ($1M in 2023; $1M in 2024; $2M in 2025).
* **Equity Investment:** Tesla invested $2 billion into xAI Series E Preferred Stock in early 2026, which converted into 3.8 million shares of SpaceX Class A Common Stock (pre-split basis) following the xAI Merger.

**Transactions with Other Musk-Affiliated Entities:**
* **The Boring Company:** X leased office space from a Boring Company subsidiary. Additionally, SpaceX incurred $1 million in 2025 for tunnel construction by The Boring Company in Bastrop, Texas.
* **Musk Industries LLC:** xAI leased real property from this Musk-owned entity ($0.5M in 2024; $2M in 2025).
* **Musk's Security Company:** SpaceX paid a Musk-owned security firm for personal security services related to his SpaceX duties ($2M in 2023; $3M in 2024; $4M in 2025).
* **Craft Aviation Services:** A Musk affiliate that maintains and services one of the corporate aircraft owned by SpaceX's subsidiary, Falcon Landing, LLC.

**Transactions with Board Members (Valor Equity Partners):**
* **Massive Compute Leases:** xAI subsidiaries entered into three equipment lease agreements with Valor Equity Partners (founded by SpaceX board member Antonio J. Gracias) for computing equipment. The aggregate cash payments over the life of these leases are massive: **$6.98 billion, $6.63 billion, and $6.58 billion**. These obligations are guaranteed by SpaceX.
* **X API Services:** X received minor payments from Valor for API services ($1M in 2024; $1M in 2025).

**Executive Aircraft Arrangements:**
* SpaceX reimburses Musk for the use of his personal aircraft for SpaceX business when corporate aircraft are unavailable.
* SpaceX executives Gwynne Shotwell and Bret Johnsen co-own a personal aircraft that is occasionally used for business purposes when corporate aircraft are unavailable.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve more information about the specific transparency and governance concerns, including any risk factors about the combined entity's financial reporting, and the MD&A discussion about how the recast affects comparability.

arguments.prompt: What does the SpaceX S-1 say about the non-GAAP financial measures, Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation, and what adjustments are made? What costs are excluded from Adjusted EBITDA? {{spacex_s1}}
What risk factors does the SpaceX S-1 disclose about Elon Musk's control, conflicts of interest, governance, controlled company status, or concentration of voting power? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here is a detailed breakdown of what the document says regarding non-GAAP financial measures, the reconciliations, the adjustments made, and the specific costs excluded.

### 1. Overview of Non-GAAP Financial Measures & Reconciliations
The S-1 states that **Adjusted EBITDA** and **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** are non-GAAP supplemental measures. 
* **Purpose:** Management uses them to assess financial performance, evaluate operating results across periods, and for internal planning and forecasting. They are also provided to investors to help evaluate core operating performance by removing the impact of items that management believes do not directly reflect ongoing operations.
* **Limitations:** The filing includes standard SEC warnings that these measures should not be viewed in isolation or as a substitute for GAAP metrics (like Net Income or Income from Operations). Furthermore, other companies may calculate these metrics differently, which limits their usefulness for direct comparison.
* **Reconciliations Provided:** The S-1 provides comprehensive tables reconciling the most directly comparable GAAP measures to the non-GAAP measures for the full years 2023, 2024, and 2025, as well as the first quarters (three months ended March 31) of 2025 and 2026:
  * **Consolidated Level:** Reconciles **Net income (loss)** to **Adjusted EBITDA**.
  * **Segment Level:** Reconciles **Income (loss) from operations** to **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** for the three reportable segments: *Space, Connectivity,* and *AI*.

### 2. What Adjustments Are Made?
The S-1 explicitly defines the formulas for the adjustments based on the starting GAAP metric:

* **Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA** is defined as *Net income (loss)* excluding eight specific categories of expenses, incomes, and taxes.
* **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** is defined as *Segment income (loss) from operations* excluding four specific categories of operating expenses. (Because it starts at the operating income level, interest and taxes are already excluded from the baseline).

### 3. What Costs and Items Are Excluded?
To arrive at the non-GAAP figures, the following costs and items are added back to (or deducted from) the GAAP baseline:

#### Excluded from Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA:
1. **Depreciation and amortization** (e.g., depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs, GPU and cloud computing depreciation).
2. **Share-based compensation**.
3. **Impairments** (Notably, the S-1 highlights a massive $3.77 billion impairment in 2023 related to the write-down of the Twitter brand intangible asset following its rebranding to X).
4. **Restructuring charges** (e.g., workforce-related restructuring costs).
5. **Interest expense**.
6. **Interest income**.
7. **Other income (expense), net**.
8. **Provision for (benefit from) income taxes**.

#### Excluded from Segment Adjusted EBITDA:
Because segment performance is evaluated at the operating level, only the following four operating-related costs are excluded from *Segment Income (loss) from operations*:
1. **Depreciation and amortization**
2. **Share-based compensation**
3. **Restructuring charges**
4. **Impairment**

### Summary of Segment Reconciliation Highlights
The reconciliation tables reveal how these excluded costs impact the different arms of the business:
* **Space Segment:** Adjusted EBITDA is heavily impacted by the add-back of depreciation/amortization and share-based compensation. The segment's operating losses in recent periods are largely driven by accelerated R&D and capital investments in Starship infrastructure, which are reflected in operating expenses and depreciation.
* **Connectivity Segment (Starlink):** Shows strong positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA, with large add-backs for depreciation and amortization (reflecting the massive capital expenditure of satellite constellations and user terminals).
* **AI Segment (xAI):** Shows significant operating losses and negative Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The adjustments highlight massive depreciation (from cloud computing and GPU infrastructure) and share-based compensation. In 2023, the AI segment also absorbed the $3.77 billion impairment charge related to the Twitter/X rebranding.

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, the document discloses extensive risk factors and structural provisions regarding Elon Musk’s control, corporate governance, conflicts of interest, and the rights of minority shareholders. 

Here is a detailed breakdown of the disclosed risks and governance structures:

### 1. Concentration of Voting Power & Dual-Class Structure
*   **Dual-Class Stock:** SpaceX will utilize a dual-class structure where Class B common stock holds **10 votes per share**, while Class A common stock (offered to the public) holds **1 vote per share**.
*   **Musk’s Absolute Control:** Post-IPO, Elon Musk will beneficially own a majority of the Class B shares and the combined voting power of the company. 
*   **Board Election & Removal:** Holders of Class B stock, voting separately as a class, are entitled to elect **51% of the board of directors** ("Class B Directors"). Furthermore, Musk can only be removed from the board or his roles as CEO and Chairman by an affirmative vote of a majority of the Class B shareholders.
*   **Minority Shareholder Limitations:** This extreme concentration of voting power will limit or entirely preclude Class A shareholders from influencing corporate matters, electing directors, or forcing changes in management.
*   **Conversion Mechanics:** Class B shares generally convert to Class A shares upon transfer (with exceptions for certain family/entity transfers). Over time, this conversion mechanism will increase the relative voting power of those who retain their Class B shares (namely, Musk).

### 2. "Controlled Company" Status & Governance Exemptions
Because Musk will hold greater than 50% of the voting power for director elections, SpaceX will qualify as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules. Consequently, SpaceX intends to rely on exemptions that allow it to bypass standard corporate governance requirements, including:
*   **No Independent Majority:** The board is *not* required to consist of a majority of independent directors.
*   **No Independent Nominating/Compensation Committees:** Director nominees do not need to be selected by an independent nominating committee, and the compensation committee does not need to be composed entirely of independent directors.
*   **No Annual Evaluations:** The company is exempt from conducting annual performance evaluations of its compensation and nominating committees.
*   *Note:* SpaceX will still be required to maintain an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors.

### 3. Conflicts of Interest & Related-Party Transactions
*   **Competition & Corporate Opportunities:** Musk and his affiliates (e.g., Tesla, xAI, Neuralink, The Boring Company) may compete directly or indirectly with SpaceX. SpaceX’s charter explicitly **renounces certain corporate opportunities** for Musk and specific directors, meaning they have no legal duty to present competing business opportunities to SpaceX.
*   **Shared Directors & Employees:** Certain SpaceX directors and key employees also serve on the boards of, or have financial ties to, other space, AI, and technology ventures affiliated with Musk. This creates actual or perceived conflicts regarding the allocation of time, resources, and strategic decisions.
*   **Intercompany Transactions:** SpaceX engages in strategic collaborations with Tesla (specifically regarding projects named "Macrohard" and "Terafab"). The S-1 warns that SpaceX may pursue transactions with Musk-affiliated entities instead of more accretive opportunities preferred by minority shareholders.
*   **Aircraft & Maintenance Expenses:** SpaceX pays a Musk affiliate (Craft Aviation Services, LLC) for aircraft maintenance (ranging from $1M to $3M annually between 2023 and early 2026). Additionally, SpaceX reimburses Musk for the use of his personal aircraft for business purposes (up to $3M in 2024).
*   **Underwriter Conflicts:** Several underwriters have existing financial conflicts, serving as lenders in SpaceX's Bridge Loan and Credit Facility, and advising on the xAI acquisition.

### 4. Key Person Risk & Divided Attention
*   **Extreme Dependence on Musk:** SpaceX’s "intense, mission-driven, engineering-first culture" and operational success are heavily tied to Musk’s vision and expertise. His loss (due to death, disability, or departure) could severely disrupt the company's strategy and stakeholder relationships.
*   **No Key-Person Insurance:** SpaceX does not maintain key-person life insurance on Elon Musk.
*   **Divided Attention:** Musk does not devote his full time to SpaceX. He holds significant roles in other ventures (e.g., "Technoking" and CEO of Tesla, Neuralink, The Boring Company), which divides his attention and resources.

### 5. Anti-Takeover Protections
Musk’s voting control, combined with specific charter provisions, creates strong anti-takeover defenses that could deter hostile buyouts or changes in control:
*   **No Cumulative Voting:** Shareholders cannot pool their votes to elect a specific director.
*   **Special Meeting Thresholds:** Special shareholder meetings can only be called by the Chairman, CEO, President, the Board, the Founder (Musk), or shareholders holding **not less than 50%** of the voting stock.
*   **Written Consent:** Actions by written consent require the minimum number of votes that would be needed to pass the action at a formal meeting, effectively giving Musk veto power over unilateral shareholder actions.

### 6. Litigation Involving Musk
*   The S-1 discloses ongoing litigation related to statements Musk made in **May 2022** concerning his potential acquisition of Twitter, Inc. The case involves allegations of a "scheme to defraud," with post-trial motions and class decertification motions expected to be heard in **June 2026**. This litigation poses a reputational and financial distraction risk tied directly to the CEO.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve more information about the specific financial data to understand the magnitude of the recast's impact - particularly how much of the historical losses are attributable to xAI/X versus SpaceX standalone.

arguments.prompt: What are the consolidated net income or loss figures for SpaceX for 2023, 2024, 2025 and Q1 2026? What is the total revenue breakdown by year? What are the total operating expenses? {{spacex_s1}}

What does the MD&A section say about year-over-year changes in revenue, operating results, and how the xAI merger affected comparability of results? What does it say about the AI segment losses and their impact on consolidated results? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided consolidated financial statements, here are the requested financial figures for SpaceX (all figures are in millions):

### **Consolidated Net Income (Loss)**
* **2023:** $(4,628) million
* **2024:** $791 million
* **2025:** $(4,937) million
* **Q1 2026** *(Three months ended March 31, 2026)*: $(4,276) million

### **Total Revenue**
* **2023:** $10,387 million
* **2024:** $14,015 million
* **2025:** $18,674 million
* **Q1 2026:** $4,694 million

### **Total Operating Expenses** 
*(Listed as "Total costs and expenses" in the statements, which includes Cost of revenue, R&D, SG&A, Restructuring charges, and Impairment)*
* **2023:** $13,892 million
* **2024:** $13,549 million
* **2025:** $21,263 million
* **Q1 2026:** $6,637 million

Based on the provided MD&A and financial statement excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of the year-over-year changes in revenue, operating results, the impact of the xAI merger on comparability, and the effects of AI segment losses on the consolidated company.

### 1. Year-Over-Year Changes in Revenue
The MD&A details revenue fluctuations across three distinct periods, highlighting a divergence between the highly profitable Connectivity (Starlink) segment and the evolving AI and Space segments:
*   **2024 vs. 2023:** Consolidated revenue growth was driven by the Connectivity segment (up $3.73 billion due to Starlink subscriber and enterprise/government growth) and the Space segment (up $239 million due to increased Falcon 9 launches). However, this was partially offset by a **$341 million (11.5%) decline in the AI segment**, driven by a massive $595 million drop in X (formerly Twitter) advertising revenue due to lost ad partners, which was only partially offset by growth in X subscriptions and data licensing.
*   **2025 vs. 2024:** The AI segment rebounded with a **$581 million (22.2%) revenue increase**, fueled by a recovery in X advertising ($116 million), surging Grok and X subscription revenue ($365 million), and data licensing ($88 million). The Connectivity segment continued its massive growth (up $3.78 billion), and Space revenue grew by $290 million driven by government contracts (e.g., NASA CRS missions).
*   **Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025:** Consolidated revenue increased by $627 million (15.4%). Connectivity added $782 million, and the AI segment added $91 million (driven by subscriptions, though offset by a temporary $100 million dip in ad revenue due to an advertising platform overhaul). Space revenue declined by $246 million due to launch timing.

### 2. Year-Over-Year Changes in Operating Results
Operating results have been heavily influenced by impairment charges, infrastructure investments, and restructuring:
*   **2024 Consolidated Improvement:** The consolidated loss from operations *decreased* by $2.41 billion (a 60.7% improvement) compared to 2023. This was primarily because 2023 included a massive $3.77 billion impairment charge related to the rebranding of the Twitter intangible asset to X, which did not recur in 2024.
*   **2025 AI Segment Deterioration:** The AI segment's loss from operations skyrocketed, **increasing by $4.79 billion (307.1%)** compared to 2024. 
*   **Q1 2026 Consolidated Decline:** Consolidated income (loss) from operations **decreased by $1.97 billion** compared to Q1 2025. Despite strong top-line growth in Starlink, the bottom line was severely dragged down by accelerated R&D and capital expenditures in both the AI segment (data centers/GPUs) and the Space segment (Starship facilities).

### 3. How the xAI Merger Affected Comparability of Results
The text explicitly notes that the acquisitions of X Holdings (by xAI in March 2025) and xAI (by SpaceX in February 2026) were transactions between entities under **common control** (majority voting interest held by Elon Musk). 
*   **Retrospective Combination:** To ensure historical comparability, the consolidated financial statements have been prepared using a **retrospective combination**. This means the historical financial and operating data of SpaceX, xAI, and X have been combined for *all periods presented* as if they had always been a single entity.
*   **Accounting Treatment:** The net assets were combined at their historical carrying amounts. **No new goodwill or intangible assets were recorded** as a result of the mergers, and all historical related-party transactions between the entities were eliminated. Consequently, the year-over-year comparisons are directly comparable and reflect the unified operational reality of the combined enterprise.

### 4. AI Segment Losses and Their Impact on Consolidated Results
The AI segment is currently operating at a massive loss, which is fundamentally altering the consolidated company's financial profile, cash flows, and cost structure:
*   **Drivers of AI Losses:** The explosive growth in AI operating losses (e.g., the $4.79 billion increase in 2025) is driven by aggressive infrastructure build-out and legacy costs. 
    *   *R&D Expenses:* Surged by $3.88 billion in 2025 (and $1.47 billion in Q1 2026 alone) due to massive GPU hardware depreciation, cloud computing costs, and AI data center expansions.
    *   *SG&A Expenses:* Increased by $722 million in 2025 due to employee compensation for AI expansion and $189 million in higher legal expenses.
    *   *Restructuring:* Ongoing charges related to the 2022 Twitter workforce reduction program continued to impact results (e.g., a $274 million increase in 2025 due to settlement adjustments with former employees).
*   **Impact on Consolidated Profitability:** The AI segment's losses are acting as a heavy anchor on consolidated operating income. Even though the Connectivity segment (Starlink) is generating substantial revenue and operating efficiently, the sheer scale of AI infrastructure spending dilutes overall consolidated profitability. 
*   **Impact on Capital Allocation & Financing:** The AI segment's capital expenditure requirements are staggering ($12.7 billion in 2025 and $7.7 billion in Q1 2026). To fund this, the consolidated entity has had to drastically alter its financing activities, relying on massive debt issuances (e.g., $16 billion in AI segment debt proceeds in 2025, and a $17.95 billion SpaceX Bridge Loan in Q1 2026) and capital stock sales to support the AI data center build-out.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the filing. Let me also look for any specific disclosure about the challenges of evaluating standalone performance or any pro forma information.

arguments.prompt: Does the SpaceX S-1 provide any pro forma financial information, standalone financial statements for SpaceX without xAI, or any supplemental disclosure showing what SpaceX's results would have been without the xAI merger? {{spacex_s1}}
What does the SpaceX S-1 say about the use of proceeds from the IPO? How will the offering proceeds be used? Will they fund the AI segment? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, **no**, the document does not provide standalone financial statements for SpaceX without xAI, nor does it provide supplemental pro forma disclosure showing what SpaceX's results would have been without the xAI merger. 

Here is why, based on the text:

**1. Common Control Accounting Requires Retrospective Combination**
The S-1 explicitly states that the acquisitions of xAI and X Holdings were accounted for as **"transactions between entities under common control"** (because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in all entities). Under this accounting treatment, the company is required to retrospectively combine the financial statements. 
* *Quote:* "The summary historical consolidated financial and operating data of SpaceX has been prepared to reflect the retrospective combination of the companies for all periods presented to include the historical results of xAI... and X Holdings..."
* *Quote:* "The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include (i) the historical results of X.AI Holdings Corp... and X Holdings Corp... This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."

Because they are treated as a single reporting entity for all historical periods presented, the SEC does not require (and the company has not provided) "standalone" carve-out financials or "what-if" supplemental disclosures excluding xAI. 

**2. The "Pro Forma" Information is for the IPO, Not for Excluding xAI**
While the document *does* contain "Pro Forma" columns and calculations (such as in the Capitalization table and the Pro Forma net loss per share computation), these are strictly related to the capital structure changes resulting from the IPO. 
* The pro forma adjustments reflect the **conversion of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** into common stock, the **Class C Reclassification**, the **2026 Stock Split**, and the effectiveness of the new corporate charter. 
* They do not adjust for or strip out the historical financial results of xAI or X. 

In summary, the S-1 presents SpaceX, xAI, and X as a single, unified historical financial entity. Investors are directed to read the consolidated financial statements and the MD&A, which encompass the combined operations of the aerospace, social media (X), and AI (xAI/Grok) segments without offering a standalone, pre-merger view of SpaceX's legacy aerospace business.

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 prospectus, here is how the company plans to use the proceeds from the IPO, including the specific implications for the AI segment:

### 1. Intended Use of Proceeds (Growth Strategy)
The S-1 explicitly states that the net proceeds from the IPO will be used to fund the company's overarching growth strategy. The specific allocations include:
*   **AI Infrastructure:** The expansion of AI compute infrastructure.
*   **Space & Launch:** Enhancements to launch infrastructure and launch vehicles.
*   **Connectivity (Starlink):** Increases in the scale and capacity of satellite constellations.
*   **General Corporate Purposes:** Any remaining amounts will be used for general corporate needs. 

### 2. Mandatory Debt Repayment (The SpaceX Bridge Loan)
While the company outlines its growth strategy, it is also bound by strict debt covenants. The prospectus notes that under the terms of the **SpaceX Bridge Loan**, the company is legally required to use the net proceeds of a "qualified initial public offering" to repay outstanding amounts under this specific loan **within six months** following receipt of the funds. 
* *Note:* The Bridge Loan is guaranteed by X Corp., X.AI LLC, and CTC Property LLC, and was heavily utilized to fund the company's AI segment and data center build-outs. Therefore, using IPO proceeds to pay off this bridge loan effectively refinances the capital already sunk into the AI and space infrastructure.

### 3. Will they fund the AI segment?
**Yes, absolutely.** The AI segment is a primary beneficiary of the IPO proceeds, both directly and indirectly:
*   **Direct Mention:** The "Use of Proceeds" section explicitly lists **"the expansion of our AI compute infrastructure"** as a primary pillar of the growth strategy funded by the offering.
*   **Massive Capital Expenditure (CapEx) Needs:** The financial tables highlight an aggressive, capital-intensive ramp-up in the AI segment. AI capital expenditures skyrocketed from $463 million in 2023 to **$12.7 billion in 2025**, and reached **$7.7 billion in just the first quarter of 2026**. 
*   **Refinancing AI Debt:** The cash flow statements show that the company has been relying heavily on debt financing specifically tied to the AI segment (e.g., the xAI Floating Rate Loan, xAI Senior Secured Notes, and the SpaceX Bridge Loan). Applying IPO proceeds to pay down the Bridge Loan frees up the company's balance sheet and liquidity to continue sustaining the massive data center and compute build-outs required for its AI segment (which includes the Grok frontier model and X platform). 

**Summary:** 
While a significant portion of the cash raised will be legally mandated to pay off the SpaceX Bridge Loan within six months, the S-1 makes it clear that the overarching purpose of this capital raise is to sustain the company's massive, multi-front growth strategy—specifically the rapid and highly capital-intensive expansion of its **AI compute infrastructure**, alongside its rocket and Starlink satellite networks. Management also retains the flexibility to reallocate spending between segments (e.g., Space, Connectivity, and AI) based on business priorities and macroeconomic conditions.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize all of this into a thorough analytical answer about the investor-interpretability and transparency concerns that arise from common-control recast accounting in the SpaceX S-1 context.

Key concerns to address:

1. **Loss of standalone observability** - Investors cannot see what SpaceX's aerospace business alone would have looked like
2. **Commingled profitability signals** - The highly profitable Connectivity segment is blended with the massively loss-making AI segment
3. **No pro forma or carve-out financials** - The filing provides no supplemental standalone disclosure
4. **Elimination of intercompany transactions** - Pre-merger commercial arrangements between SpaceX, xAI, and X are invisible
5. **Capital allocation opacity** - IPO proceeds will fund AI infrastructure, but investors can't assess the standalone returns of the legacy business
6. **Non-GAAP adjustments layered on top of recast** - Adjusted EBITDA excludes major recurring costs (depreciation of GPUs, SBC) on top of an already recast base
7. **Governance and conflict-of-interest overlay** - Musk controls all entities; controlled-company exemptions; related-party transactions with other Musk entities
8. **Bifurcated equity treatment** - Preferred stock not retrospectively recast, creating mixed presentation
9. **Cross-subsidization masking** - Inter-segment launch services not recognized as revenue, masking true economic value
10. **Historical trend distortion** - Year-over-year growth rates and margins reflect a changing mix of businesses that were never actually combined during those periods

Let me write a comprehensive answer.

arguments.prompt: What does the SpaceX S-1 disclose about inter-segment eliminations, how internal launch services are accounted for, and whether the Space segment recognizes revenue from launching Starlink satellites? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1, here is how the company discloses inter-segment eliminations, the accounting for internal launch services, and revenue recognition regarding Starlink satellites:

### 1. Inter-Segment Eliminations and Starlink Revenue Recognition
*   **No Inter-Segment Revenue for Starlink:** The S-1 explicitly states that for launches dedicated to deploying internal Starlink satellites, the Company **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue**. 
*   **Space Segment Revenue Scope:** The Space segment's revenue *only* reflects external customer launches and customer activities. Internal constellation deployments are entirely excluded from the Space segment's top-line revenue.
*   **Starship Classification:** The filing notes that a launch is only designated as a "customer launch" if an external customer payload is the primary objective. To date, all Starship launches have been classified as internal, meaning they also do not generate Space segment revenue.

### 2. Accounting Treatment for Internal Launch Services
*   **Capitalization of Costs:** Instead of recognizing revenue and a corresponding cost of sales for internal launches, the costs associated with launching Starlink satellites are **capitalized within the Connectivity segment** as part of "satellites in *Property, plant, and equipment, net*."
*   **Depreciation:** Once capitalized, these launch costs are depreciated over time alongside the satellite hardware. 
*   **Impact on Business Metrics:** Because internal launches are capitalized rather than recorded as segment revenue, the Space segment's revenue growth is artificially decoupled from the company's actual launch cadence. The S-1 explains that operational metrics like "Mass to Orbit" and total launches "grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because these metrics include our internal constellation deployments from which we do not recognize inter-segment revenue."

### 3. Future Capacity Allocations (Connectivity & AI)
*   The S-1 highlights that SpaceX currently allocates a "significant amount of launch capacity" to its Connectivity segment (for Starlink) and explicitly states that it expects to **allocate a significant amount to its AI segment in the future**. This aligns with the company's stated strategy of building "orbital AI" and in-orbit data centers. Like Starlink, these future internal AI infrastructure launches will bypass Space segment revenue recognition and be treated as capital expenditures for the AI segment.

### 4. General Revenue Recognition Policies (External Customers)
For context on how the Space segment *does* recognize revenue from external third parties, the S-1 outlines two main categories:
*   **Launch Services (Point in Time):** Revenue and costs are deferred and recognized at a single "point in time" upon the successful launch or deployment of the customer’s payload to its intended orbit. This treatment is due to the interchangeability of flight hardware and minimal unique engineering costs.
*   **Launch and Development (Over Time):** Revenue for government agency space programs and spacecraft development is recognized "over time" using the **cost-to-cost input method**. This applies because the work creates an asset with no alternative use, and SpaceX has an enforceable right to payment for performance to date. 

**Summary:** 
SpaceX treats its Space segment strictly as a third-party, customer-facing business for revenue recognition purposes. Internal launches (Starlink and future AI infrastructure) are eliminated as inter-segment transactions; their costs are instead capitalized as fixed assets on the balance sheets of the Connectivity and AI segments, where they are depreciated over their useful lives.

steps.message: Now I have comprehensive information from the filing. Let me synthesize the analysis.
steps.reasoning_content: Now I have all the information I need to write a comprehensive answer. Let me synthesize everything into a well-structured analytical response about the investor-interpretability and transparency concerns arising from common-control recast accounting in the SpaceX S-1.

arguments.final_result:
## Investor-Interpretability and Transparency Concerns Arising from Common-Control Recast Accounting in the SpaceX S-1

Common-control recast accounting under ASC 805-50 requires that entities under common control be presented as if they had always been combined. In the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), this means the historical financial statements for all periods presented (2023, 2024, 2025, and Q1 2026) retrospectively combine SpaceX, xAI (merged February 2, 2026), and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) at historical carrying amounts, with no new goodwill and all intercompany transactions eliminated. While this treatment is GAAP-compliant, it creates several material investor-interpretability and transparency concerns:

---

### 1. **Loss of Standalone Performance Observability**

The most fundamental concern is that investors cannot observe what SpaceX's legacy aerospace and connectivity businesses would have produced on a standalone basis. The filing provides **no pro forma or supplemental carve-out financials** excluding xAI/X. This is analytically significant because:

- **Consolidated net income** swung from $(4,628)M in 2023 → $791M in 2024 → $(4,937)M in 2025 → $(4,276)M in Q1 2026 alone. These swings are heavily driven by the AI segment's escalating losses (which increased $4.79 billion year-over-year in 2025), but investors cannot precisely isolate the magnitude of the drag versus the legacy business's standalone trajectory.
- The Connectivity segment generated $4,423M in operating income in FY2025 and $1,188M in Q1 2026, while the AI segment produced operating losses of $(6,355)M and $(2,469)M in the same periods. The consolidated numbers net these together, obscuring the fact that an investor buying SPCX shares at IPO is effectively funding a highly profitable satellite broadband business alongside a massively loss-making AI startup.

### 2. **Commingled Profitability Signals and Margin Distortion**

Because the recast presents all periods as a unified entity, key profitability metrics—gross margin, operating margin, EBITDA margin—reflect a **changing and undisclosed business mix** that was never actually combined during those historical periods:

- In 2023, the AI segment absorbed a $3.77 billion impairment charge (Twitter/X brand write-down), inflating consolidated operating losses to approximately $(3.5) billion. This was a legacy X Holdings event, not a SpaceX operational outcome, yet it is embedded in the recast historical record.
- Total operating expenses jumped from $13,549M in 2024 to $21,263M in 2025—a 57% increase—driven largely by AI segment R&D and infrastructure spending. Without standalone disclosure, investors cannot assess whether the legacy business's cost structure was stable or deteriorating.

### 3. **Elimination of Pre-Merger Intercompany Transactions**

The filing states that "all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation." This means any pre-merger commercial arrangements between SpaceX, xAI, and X—such as potential agreements for launch services, data sharing, compute resource sharing, or platform access—are **invisible to investors**. These eliminations:

- Remove potentially arm's-length pricing signals that could have revealed the fair-market value of cross-entity services.
- Prevent investors from assessing whether the pre-merger entities were commercially interdependent or operationally independent.
- Make it impossible to evaluate whether the merger created genuine synergies or merely formalized existing below-market transfers.

### 4. **Inter-Segment Cross-Subsidization Masking**

Even within the recast segment reporting, the Space segment **does not recognize inter-segment revenue** for internal Starlink or future AI infrastructure launches. Instead, launch costs are capitalized as PP&E in the Connectivity and AI segments. This creates a structural transparency gap:

- The Space segment's reported revenue ($4,086M in Q1 2026) reflects only external customer launches, understating the segment's true economic contribution to the enterprise.
- The filing acknowledges that operational metrics like "Mass to Orbit" and total launches "grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because these metrics include our internal constellation deployments."
- Investors cannot determine the implicit subsidy the Space segment provides to Connectivity and AI, nor can they assess what the Space segment's standalone revenue and margins would be if internal launches were priced at market rates.

### 5. **Capital Allocation Opacity and IPO Proceeds**

The S-1 states IPO proceeds will fund "expansion of our AI compute infrastructure" alongside space and connectivity investments. AI segment capital expenditures were $12.7 billion in FY2025 and $7.7 billion in Q1 2026 alone. Because standalone financials are unavailable:

- Investors cannot assess the returns on capital historically deployed in the legacy aerospace business versus the AI segment.
- The mandatory repayment of the $17.95 billion SpaceX Bridge Loan (which funded AI infrastructure) from IPO proceeds effectively means public investors are refinancing capital already sunk into xAI's data centers—without having had visibility into the standalone risk-return profile of that investment.
- Management retains discretion to reallocate spending across segments, and the recast accounting provides no baseline for evaluating whether capital is being deployed to its highest-return use.

### 6. **Non-GAAP Adjustments Layered on an Already Opaque Base**

Segment Adjusted EBITDA excludes depreciation, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairments from segment operating income. When layered on top of recast accounting:

- The AI segment's Adjusted EBITDA was $(609)M in Q1 2026 and $(1,237)M in FY2025—but these figures exclude massive GPU depreciation and SBC that are core, recurring economic costs of the AI business.
- The Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087M in Q1 2026 excludes satellite depreciation, which is a genuine recurring capital consumption cost.
- Investors must navigate a **double layer of opacity**: recast accounting that commingles fundamentally different businesses, plus non-GAAP metrics that exclude the largest cost drivers of the most capital-intensive segments.

### 7. **Bifurcated Equity Presentation**

The recast creates an internally inconsistent equity presentation:
- **Common stock** of X, xAI, and SpaceX is retroactively recast on an as-converted basis (with exchange ratios and a 5-for-1 stock split applied to all periods).
- **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** is *not* retrospectively recast—it remains in its historical legal form on the balance sheet until the Q1 2026 merger date, when it converts prospectively.

This bifurcation means historical earnings-per-share and book-value-per-share calculations reflect a hybrid capital structure that does not correspond to any actual historical legal entity, complicating valuation analysis.

### 8. **Governance and Conflict-of-Interest Overlay**

The recast accounting concerns are amplified by governance structures:
- SpaceX qualifies as a **"controlled company"** and will rely on exemptions from Nasdaq rules requiring a majority-independent board, independent nominating and compensation committees.
- Elon Musk held controlling interests in all merged entities, meaning the common-control determination itself rests on a single individual's overlapping fiduciary roles.
- Related-party transactions with other Musk entities (Tesla, Boring Company, Valor Equity Partners) continue post-merger, including $6.98B + $6.63B + $6.58B in compute equipment leases with Valor (guaranteed by SpaceX).
- The charter explicitly **renounces corporate opportunities** for Musk and certain directors, meaning competing ventures (e.g., Tesla's AI investments) need not be offered to SpaceX.

Without standalone financials, investors cannot assess whether the merger terms, intercompany eliminations, and ongoing related-party transactions were structured to benefit SpaceX public shareholders or to optimize the broader Musk ecosystem.

### 9. **Historical Trend Analysis Becomes Unreliable**

Standard equity analysis relies on multi-year trend analysis of revenue growth, margin expansion, and capital efficiency. The recast fundamentally undermines this:

- Revenue grew from $10,387M (2023) to $18,674M (2025), but this growth trajectory blends the maturation of Starlink, the decline and partial recovery of X advertising revenue (down $595M in 2024, rebounding $581M in 2025), and the nascent ramp of Grok subscriptions.
- Year-over-year operating expense growth of 57% in 2025 reflects the AI infrastructure buildout, not organic cost inflation in the legacy business.
- Investors applying standard DCF or comparable-company analysis to the consolidated entity are effectively valuing three fundamentally different businesses (mature aerospace, scaling broadband, early-stage AI) using a single blended historical dataset that never existed as a combined economic unit.

---

### Summary

The common-control recast in the SpaceX S-1 is GAAP-compliant but creates a **structural transparency deficit** for public-market investors. The absence of standalone financials, the elimination of pre-merger intercompany transactions, the inter-segment cross-subsidization masking, the layering of non-GAAP adjustments on a recast base, and the governance concentration around a single controlling shareholder collectively make it difficult for investors to:

1. Assess the standalone risk-return profile of the legacy aerospace and connectivity businesses
2. Evaluate the true cost and capital intensity of the AI segment as a distinct investment
3. Determine whether IPO proceeds are being allocated to their highest-return use
4. Perform reliable multi-year trend analysis or peer comparability analysis
5. Assess whether the merger terms and ongoing related-party arrangements are fair to minority shareholders

The filing's own cautionary language—that historical data "is not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period"—underscores that the recast financial statements represent an accounting construct rather than an observable economic history.

---

**Sources:**

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (Filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Investor-Interpretability and Transparency Concerns Arising from Common-Control Recast Accounting in the SpaceX S-1

Common-control recast accounting under ASC 805-50 requires that entities under common control be presented as if they had always been combined. In the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), this means the historical financial statements for all periods presented (2023, 2024, 2025, and Q1 2026) retrospectively combine SpaceX, xAI (merged February 2, 2026), and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) at historical carrying amounts, with no new goodwill and all intercompany transactions eliminated. While this treatment is GAAP-compliant, it creates several material investor-interpretability and transparency concerns:

---

### 1. **Loss of Standalone Performance Observability**

The most fundamental concern is that investors cannot observe what SpaceX's legacy aerospace and connectivity businesses would have produced on a standalone basis. The filing provides **no pro forma or supplemental carve-out financials** excluding xAI/X. This is analytically significant because:

- **Consolidated net income** swung from $(4,628)M in 2023 → $791M in 2024 → $(4,937)M in 2025 → $(4,276)M in Q1 2026 alone. These swings are heavily driven by the AI segment's escalating losses (which increased $4.79 billion year-over-year in 2025), but investors cannot precisely isolate the magnitude of the drag versus the legacy business's standalone trajectory.
- The Connectivity segment generated $4,423M in operating income in FY2025 and $1,188M in Q1 2026, while the AI segment produced operating losses of $(6,355)M and $(2,469)M in the same periods. The consolidated numbers net these together, obscuring the fact that an investor buying SPCX shares at IPO is effectively funding a highly profitable satellite broadband business alongside a massively loss-making AI startup.

### 2. **Commingled Profitability Signals and Margin Distortion**

Because the recast presents all periods as a unified entity, key profitability metrics—gross margin, operating margin, EBITDA margin—reflect a **changing and undisclosed business mix** that was never actually combined during those historical periods:

- In 2023, the AI segment absorbed a $3.77 billion impairment charge (Twitter/X brand write-down), inflating consolidated operating losses to approximately $(3.5) billion. This was a legacy X Holdings event, not a SpaceX operational outcome, yet it is embedded in the recast historical record.
- Total operating expenses jumped from $13,549M in 2024 to $21,263M in 2025—a 57% increase—driven largely by AI segment R&D and infrastructure spending. Without standalone disclosure, investors cannot assess whether the legacy business's cost structure was stable or deteriorating.

### 3. **Elimination of Pre-Merger Intercompany Transactions**

The filing states that "all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation." This means any pre-merger commercial arrangements between SpaceX, xAI, and X—such as potential agreements for launch services, data sharing, compute resource sharing, or platform access—are **invisible to investors**. These eliminations:

- Remove potentially arm's-length pricing signals that could have revealed the fair-market value of cross-entity services.
- Prevent investors from assessing whether the pre-merger entities were commercially interdependent or operationally independent.
- Make it impossible to evaluate whether the merger created genuine synergies or merely formalized existing below-market transfers.

### 4. **Inter-Segment Cross-Subsidization Masking**

Even within the recast segment reporting, the Space segment **does not recognize inter-segment revenue** for internal Starlink or future AI infrastructure launches. Instead, launch costs are capitalized as PP&E in the Connectivity and AI segments. This creates a structural transparency gap:

- The Space segment's reported revenue ($4,086M in Q1 2026) reflects only external customer launches, understating the segment's true economic contribution to the enterprise.
- The filing acknowledges that operational metrics like "Mass to Orbit" and total launches "grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because these metrics include our internal constellation deployments."
- Investors cannot determine the implicit subsidy the Space segment provides to Connectivity and AI, nor can they assess what the Space segment's standalone revenue and margins would be if internal launches were priced at market rates.

### 5. **Capital Allocation Opacity and IPO Proceeds**

The S-1 states IPO proceeds will fund "expansion of our AI compute infrastructure" alongside space and connectivity investments. AI segment capital expenditures were $12.7 billion in FY2025 and $7.7 billion in Q1 2026 alone. Because standalone financials are unavailable:

- Investors cannot assess the returns on capital historically deployed in the legacy aerospace business versus the AI segment.
- The mandatory repayment of the $17.95 billion SpaceX Bridge Loan (which funded AI infrastructure) from IPO proceeds effectively means public investors are refinancing capital already sunk into xAI's data centers—without having had visibility into the standalone risk-return profile of that investment.
- Management retains discretion to reallocate spending across segments, and the recast accounting provides no baseline for evaluating whether capital is being deployed to its highest-return use.

### 6. **Non-GAAP Adjustments Layered on an Already Opaque Base**

Segment Adjusted EBITDA excludes depreciation, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairments from segment operating income. When layered on top of recast accounting:

- The AI segment's Adjusted EBITDA was $(609)M in Q1 2026 and $(1,237)M in FY2025—but these figures exclude massive GPU depreciation and SBC that are core, recurring economic costs of the AI business.
- The Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087M in Q1 2026 excludes satellite depreciation, which is a genuine recurring capital consumption cost.
- Investors must navigate a **double layer of opacity**: recast accounting that commingles fundamentally different businesses, plus non-GAAP metrics that exclude the largest cost drivers of the most capital-intensive segments.

### 7. **Bifurcated Equity Presentation**

The recast creates an internally inconsistent equity presentation:
- **Common stock** of X, xAI, and SpaceX is retroactively recast on an as-converted basis (with exchange ratios and a 5-for-1 stock split applied to all periods).
- **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** is *not* retrospectively recast—it remains in its historical legal form on the balance sheet until the Q1 2026 merger date, when it converts prospectively.

This bifurcation means historical earnings-per-share and book-value-per-share calculations reflect a hybrid capital structure that does not correspond to any actual historical legal entity, complicating valuation analysis.

### 8. **Governance and Conflict-of-Interest Overlay**

The recast accounting concerns are amplified by governance structures:
- SpaceX qualifies as a **"controlled company"** and will rely on exemptions from Nasdaq rules requiring a majority-independent board, independent nominating and compensation committees.
- Elon Musk held controlling interests in all merged entities, meaning the common-control determination itself rests on a single individual's overlapping fiduciary roles.
- Related-party transactions with other Musk entities (Tesla, Boring Company, Valor Equity Partners) continue post-merger, including $6.98B + $6.63B + $6.58B in compute equipment leases with Valor (guaranteed by SpaceX).
- The charter explicitly **renounces corporate opportunities** for Musk and certain directors, meaning competing ventures (e.g., Tesla's AI investments) need not be offered to SpaceX.

Without standalone financials, investors cannot assess whether the merger terms, intercompany eliminations, and ongoing related-party transactions were structured to benefit SpaceX public shareholders or to optimize the broader Musk ecosystem.

### 9. **Historical Trend Analysis Becomes Unreliable**

Standard equity analysis relies on multi-year trend analysis of revenue growth, margin expansion, and capital efficiency. The recast fundamentally undermines this:

- Revenue grew from $10,387M (2023) to $18,674M (2025), but this growth trajectory blends the maturation of Starlink, the decline and partial recovery of X advertising revenue (down $595M in 2024, rebounding $581M in 2025), and the nascent ramp of Grok subscriptions.
- Year-over-year operating expense growth of 57% in 2025 reflects the AI infrastructure buildout, not organic cost inflation in the legacy business.
- Investors applying standard DCF or comparable-company analysis to the consolidated entity are effectively valuing three fundamentally different businesses (mature aerospace, scaling broadband, early-stage AI) using a single blended historical dataset that never existed as a combined economic unit.

---

### Summary

The common-control recast in the SpaceX S-1 is GAAP-compliant but creates a **structural transparency deficit** for public-market investors. The absence of standalone financials, the elimination of pre-merger intercompany transactions, the inter-segment cross-subsidization masking, the layering of non-GAAP adjustments on a recast base, and the governance concentration around a single controlling shareholder collectively make it difficult for investors to:

1. Assess the standalone risk-return profile of the legacy aerospace and connectivity businesses
2. Evaluate the true cost and capital intensity of the AI segment as a distinct investment
3. Determine whether IPO proceeds are being allocated to their highest-return use
4. Perform reliable multi-year trend analysis or peer comparability analysis
5. Assess whether the merger terms and ongoing related-party arrangements are fair to minority shareholders

The filing's own cautionary language—that historical data "is not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period"—underscores that the recast financial statements represent an accounting construct rather than an observable economic history.

---
